html:
Getting a Damaged Roof Dry Before the Next Round of Weather

When a storm opens a hole in a commercial roof, the priority isn't a permanent fix — it's keeping water out of the building until a proper repair or replacement can be scoped and scheduled. That's what emergency tarping and dry-in service is for, and doing it right in the first few hours after damage matters as much as the tarp itself.

What Counts as an Emergency on a Commercial Roof

Active water intrusion into occupied space is the clearest case — a torn membrane over a retail sales floor, a distribution warehouse with product exposed to a leak, a medical building where water is tracking toward equipment or patient areas. Wind-lifted membrane that's flapping and at risk of tearing further also qualifies, even before water has actually entered the building, because the exposure only gets worse with the next gust or the next rain band.

The First Hours After a Storm Opens a Roof

Speed matters, but so does doing the assessment correctly under pressure. We identify the actual extent of the opening — more than the visible hole alone, including how far wind may have lifted membrane beyond it — before deciding how much area needs to be covered. A tarp that's too small or poorly secured can fail in the next wind event and leave the building worse off than before the temporary fix.

Securing loose debris on the roof is part of this too. A storm that's already torn one section of membrane often leaves loose material nearby that becomes its own hazard in continued wind, either tearing further or becoming debris that damages adjacent equipment.

We also check adjacent rooftop equipment while we're up there. A storm strong enough to open a section of membrane has often loosened equipment covers, condensate lines, or exhaust hoods nearby, and those are easy to miss on a rushed call focused only on the visible hole.

Tarping Right vs. Tarping Fast

A tarp thrown over a hole and weighted with a few sandbags looks like a fix from the ground. Whether it actually holds through the next storm cell depends on how it's secured at the edges, whether it's sealed against the existing membrane to prevent wind from getting underneath and lifting the whole thing, and whether it's sized to actually cover the full extent of the damage rather than just the visible center of it. We've re-tarped roofs where another crew's rushed work failed within days of the original storm.

  • Full assessment of damage extent, including wind-lifted membrane beyond the visible opening
  • Tarp sized and overlapped to cover the entire damaged area with margin
  • Edges sealed and secured against wind uplift rather than only weighted down
  • Loose debris and damaged material secured or removed from the roof
  • Photo documentation of conditions before and after the temporary repair

Emergency Response Pricing Games

Storm season brings out pricing that wouldn't survive scrutiny on a normal Tuesday. Vague "emergency service rate" language with no hourly figure attached, minimum call charges that aren't disclosed until the invoice arrives, or a tarp job priced like it's a full repair because the owner is desperate and not in a position to shop around. We quote emergency work the same way we quote everything else — a rate the owner knows before we're on the roof, not a number that gets set after the fact because the situation was urgent.

It's fair to ask any crew showing up after a storm for a number before they start, in writing if possible. A legitimate operation can give a rate structure over the phone. One that won't commit to anything until the work is already underway is usually the one padding the final invoice.

What Happens After the Tarp: Documentation and Next Steps

A tarp buys time, not a solution. Once the building is dry, the next step is a full assessment of what caused the opening and whether it's a localized repair or a sign the roof needs a broader look. We document the storm damage thoroughly at this stage — this is the record that matters most if an insurance claim follows, since it captures conditions closest to the actual event rather than weeks later.

We also walk the owner through a realistic timeline for the permanent fix at this stage, rather than leaving the tarp up indefinitely while decisions get made. A section that's been temporarily patched needs a firm date to come off the schedule, or it tends to sit longer than anyone intended once daily operations settle back to normal.

Emergency Tarp and Dry-In Questions

How fast can you respond after a storm?

We prioritize active water intrusion calls and move them ahead of scheduled work, with response time depending on how widespread the storm damage is across the area.

Is a tarp a permanent fix?

No. It's a temporary measure to stop water intrusion until a permanent repair or replacement can be properly scoped.

How long can a tarp stay up before it needs to be replaced with a real repair?

That depends on the damage and the weather that follows, but we don't recommend leaving a temporary tarp in place any longer than it takes to schedule the permanent fix.

Does emergency tarping cost more than scheduled repair work?

Emergency response typically carries a different rate structure than scheduled work, and we disclose that rate before starting, not after.

Will the tarp work count toward my insurance claim documentation?

The photos and assessment from the emergency visit become part of the documentation record, which is useful whether or not a claim is ultimately filed.
